actions.thought: I am on the button with 8h 6h. This is a playable suited connector that performs well in position. Since I am first to act, I will open-raise to 250 to attempt to take down the blinds or see a flop with the initiative.

Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button, King-Five offsuit is way too weak to call or 3-bet from the Small Blind. Being out of position post-flop with a marginal hand that is easily dominated makes folding the only viable choice.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9c 5h, which is a very weak hand. The button (Flashlight) raised to 250, and the Small Blind folded. Although I only need to call 150 more to see a flop, 95o performs poorly out of position and has very little playability. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack early in the tournament.

Holding 5d3c on the Button in a 3-handed game is an easy fold. It is an unsuited, disconnected, and weak starting hand that offers terrible playability post-flop. Folding to preserve my stack for better hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 3s, which is a very weak hand. Since the Button has folded, it's just me and the Big Blind. While I could attempt a steal or complete, 93o plays poorly post-flop and out of position. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with 5c 3d, which is a very weak hand. Standard strategy is to fold this hand pre-flop to avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop, especially early in the tournament with 100BB stacks.

With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. QTo is a strong enough starting hand to justify an aggressive approach in this position. By raising to 300, I can claim the betting initiative, maximize my fold equity against the big blind, and set myself up for a strong post-flop position. This is superior to simply limping, which would surrender the initiative.

Opponent in the Small Blind raised to 3x. I hold K5o in the Big Blind. Even though I will have position post-flop against the Small Blind, King-Five unsuited is a very weak holding with poor playability and reverse implied odds. It is frequently dominated by other King-high hands in a raising range. Defending against a 3x raise with this hand is not profitable, so the best action is to fold.

With Ace-Nine offsuit on the button, I have a clear opening opportunity. My hand is strong enough to play in position against the small and big blinds. A standard raise to 250 should be sufficient to either take down the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop scenario where I have the initiative.

Facing a 2.5x raise from the Button, A6o is a marginal hand from the Small Blind. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser and with the Big Blind still left to act, calling is unprofitable due to positional disadvantage and reverse implied odds. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but there are better hands for that in my range. Folding is the most solid, standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind holding J7o. Flashlight on the Button has raised to 250. While J7o can occasionally be a marginal defend in the BB against a wide button opening range, it is generally a weak hand to play out of position, especially this early in the tournament. Given the aggressive nature shown by Flashlight in previous hands, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a better spot rather than calling and playing a bloated pot out of position with a weak high-card hand.

K7o is a marginal hand, even from the button. While stealing is an option, it is easily dominated by any Ace, better Kings, or pocket pairs, and plays poorly post-flop. I'll fold and wait for a stronger holding since we still have a deep stack of 98 BBs.

I am in the Small Blind with Ks 2s. The Button has folded, so it is just me and the Big Blind. Ks 2s is a strong enough hand to play in a blind-versus-blind battle. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Flashlight and potentially take the pot down pre-flop, or play a pot with the initiative.

8-10 offsuit is a weak hand to defend against an open raise from the small blind, especially when playing out of position for the rest of the hand. With a long tournament ahead, preserving the stack is a priority over contesting marginal pots with a hand that will be difficult to play on most boards.

Holding Ace-Seven offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game is a standard opening range hand. I should raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. A size of 2.5x the big blind (250) is standard.

I am in the small blind with a small pocket pair (22), which has good implied odds for set-mining. Since the button raised to 250, calling 200 more is a reasonable price to see a flop. If I hit a deuce, I have a very strong hand. If I miss, I can fold to aggressive betting on the flop. A 3-bet here would be overplaying the hand out of position.

KTs is a strong suited broadway hand that plays excellently in multi-way pots. With the Button raising and the Small Blind calling, I'm getting great pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 600) to close the action and see a flop. While a 3-bet squeeze is an option, KTs performs well enough as a flat call to realize its equity while keeping the pot manageable.

I have missed the flop completely with pocket deuces on a highly connected board (8-J-9). With two opponents and being out of position, my hand has very little value and I have no equity to justify a bet. Checking is the most sensible play to keep the pot small and re-evaluate based on the action from the other players, intending to fold if there is significant aggression.

I have an open-ended straight draw (K-T on 8-9-J) and an overcard, but no flush draw on a two-spade board. Being out of position in a multi-way pot, the standard line is to check to the pre-flop raiser (Flash). This allows me to evaluate the action, potentially see a cheap turn card, and avoid getting raised off my equity if I were to lead out.

The board 8s-Jc-9s is highly coordinated and likely connects well with the ranges of both the small and big blinds. I currently hold Ace high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10) and the Ace of Spades as a relevant blocker. Since both opponents have checked, checking back is a prudent play to realize my equity and see a free turn card without risking a check-raise on such a dangerous board texture.

The King of Spades on the turn makes the board significantly more dangerous, adding a potential flush draw and pairing the King. My pocket deuces have no showdown value and no outs to improve meaningfully. Checking is the only logical play, as betting would be a pure bluff into two opponents on a board that likely hit their calling ranges. I will look to fold if there is any aggressive betting from the other players.

The turn brings a King, giving me top pair to go with my open-ended straight draw. However, the King of Spades completes a possible flush, and the board is incredibly wet and connected (8-9-J-K). With three spades out there and holding no spades myself, betting into two opponents is very risky as I will mostly be called or raised by better hands like flushes, straights, two pairs, or stronger Kings. Checking allows for pot control and gives me a chance to evaluate the action from the pre-flop raiser on the button.

The turn King of Spades is a very dynamic card, completing the flush draw and several straight possibilities on a board of 8s-Jc-9s. I hold the Ace of Spades, which is the nut flush blocker, and a gutshot straight draw to a ten. Both Flashlight and Pro have checked twice now (flop and turn). While I could attempt to bluff given their apparent weakness, checking back is a safer play to realize my equity and potentially win at showdown with Ace high or improve on the river for free. There is little reason to bloat the pot on such a coordinated board without a made hand.

The board is extremely coordinated with potential straights and a flush. My pocket deuces have no showdown value, and I lack any cards (specifically spades) to effectively represent a strong hand. Since the betting has been checked around through all streets, the pot is relatively small and no one has shown aggression. Betting as a bluff here is high-risk with low reward, as any pair or better will likely call, and I have no blockers. Checking is the optimal play to reach a free showdown.

The river brings a fourth spade to the board, making any single spade in my opponents' hands a flush. The board also has 7-8-9-J-K, and while my Ten gives me a straight (7-8-9-T-J), the four-flush completely devalues it. I do not hold a spade. Betting here is suicidal as I would only ever be called or raised by a flush or higher straight (like Queen-Ten). Checking is the safest and only logical play.

I have the nut flush with the Ace of Spades on a four-spade board (As-Ks-9s-8s-7s). Both Flashlight and Pro have checked through the flop, turn, and river, suggesting they are unlikely to have strong hands. However, since I hold the absolute nuts, I must bet for value rather than checking back. A bet of 500 into a 750 pot is large enough to extract value from any lower spade or a straight, while still potentially inducing a curious call from weaker made hands.

The opponent has bet 500 into a pot of 750 on a board that is extremely wet, featuring four spades and multiple straight possibilities. My pocket deuces have zero showdown value and cannot beat even a weak pair, let alone a flush or a straight. There is no justification for a call here.

The board has four spades and I hold no spades, meaning my Jack-high straight is severely devalued. Flash's river bet strongly represents a flush, as any single spade in his hand beats me. My hand is merely a bluff-catcher at this point, and calling a 2/3 pot bet without a spade is a losing play long-term. Folding is the disciplined choice.

Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and no suited potential. In a three-handed game, even on the button, there is no justification for playing such a low-equity hand. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

With the Button folding, the action is on me in the Small Blind with A7o. This is a strong hand for a Blind versus Blind situation. Rather than limping and playing out of position without the initiative, I will raise to 3x the big blind (300). This puts pressure on the Big Blind, often taking down the pot immediately, and gives me the betting lead if called.

I am in the big blind with KQo. The small blind has raised to 3x the big blind after the button folded. In a blind-versus-blind battle, KQo is a premium hand. I will 3-bet to 900 to take the initiative, put pressure on the small blind, and extract value from their wide opening range while having the advantage of position for the rest of the hand.

I opened with A7o from the Small Blind, which is a standard steal attempt. However, the Big Blind has responded with a 3-bet to 900. A7 offsuit is relatively weak to call a 3-bet with, especially since I will be out of position post-flop. The Big Blind's 3-bet range likely dominates my hand (better aces, strong broadways, medium/high pairs). Folding is the mathematically sound and safest option here.
Q3o is a very weak, unsuited hand that is not worth playing, even from the button. The most profitable play in the long run is to simply fold and save chips for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 7d, a weak offsuit hand. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). While I could attempt a blind steal or complete the bet, J7o is generally not strong enough to play out of position against a competent opponent. Given my current chip lead and the need to maintain a solid table image, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

J7s is a strong candidate for an open-raise from the button in a 3-handed game. I have a healthy stack and want to maintain pressure. Opening to 300 (3x BB) allows me to either take the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds if they call.

K7 offsuit is a weak holding, especially out of position in the small blind against a button open. With the big blind yet to act, calling would be risky as I could face a squeeze, and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, Q9s is a perfectly fine hand to defend from the Big Blind. It's suited, has high card value, and can make straights or flushes. I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call for a pot of 650) to see a flop heads-up, although I'll be out of position.

I missed the flop completely with Q9 of clubs on a K-5-2 rainbow board. I only have a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard and optimal play is to check to the pre-flop raiser.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and this King-high, dry board (Kd 5c 2s) favors my range significantly. Pro checked to me, and a continuation bet of 300 (roughly 46% of the pot) should be effective in folding out hands that missed. I have a backdoor flush draw and two overcards to the lower board cards.

The board is Kd 5c 2s. I have Qc 9c. I completely missed the flop, having only a backdoor flush draw and no pair or direct straight draws. My opponent (Flash) continuation bet for 300. Without any significant equity or positional advantage to bluff later streets effectively, folding is the most standard and profitable decision.

I am in the button position with King-Nine suited, a hand with decent equity and playability in a three-handed game. Opening with a raise is standard here to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds, as consistent with my strategy in previous hands.
K2o is a very weak hand to play from the small blind against a button raise. Out of position and with poor playability, folding is the standard and correct choice.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Eight offsuit (Q8o). The player on the Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250, which is a standard 2.5x open. After the Small Blind folds, I am getting attractive pot odds (150 to call into a total pot of 400) of 2.66:1. Q8o is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind against a wide button opening range, so I will call to see a flop.

On a flop of 3c-9s-6s, I have Queen-high with two overcards to the board's lower cards. This board texture is fairly dry but offers some straight and flush draw possibilities for my opponent's button-opening range. Standard play from the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I will check and see how Flashlight responds to evaluate if my overcards or potential backdoor equity are worth continuing with.

I have top pair (Nines) with a decent kicker on a board of 3c-9s-6s. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the opponent checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play to maintain initiative, build the pot, and protect my hand against potential flush or straight draws. A bet of 300 (roughly 55% of the pot) should extract value from weaker pairs or draws while keeping the pot manageable.

Flashlight has made a standard continuation bet of 300 into a 550 pot. My hand, Queen-Eight offsuit, has missed this flop entirely. While I have overcards to the 3 and 6, I only have one overcard to the 9 and no significant draws on this spade-heavy board. Calling out of position with just Queen-high and no real equity or backdoor potential is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

With A3o on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard spot to attempt a steal. Opening to 2.5x BB puts pressure on the blinds and gives me the initiative, but the hand is weak enough that I can comfortably fold if faced with a 3-bet.

J7o is a weak starting hand, especially out of position against a button raise. Calling 200 more chips into a 400 pot with this hand is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

The button has raised to 2.5BB, which is a standard opening size. Being in the big blind with Queen-Eight offsuit, I am getting sufficient pot odds to defend my blind. Folding would be too passive, and 3-betting with this specific hand is not optimal given our out-of-position disadvantage. Calling allows me to see a flop with a decent range and maintain control of my stack.

I hold middle pair on a dry, low-card board (4-8-5). As the out-of-position player against the pre-flop aggressor (the button), checking is the standard, high-frequency play. It allows me to control the pot size and observe the opponent's reaction to the flop. Checking also keeps my checking range protected while potentially inducing a c-bet from the opponent, which I can then call with my pair.

The 4s-8h-5d flop hits the Big Blind's defending range very well, as it contains many combinations of suited connectors and one-gappers (e.g., 76, 87, 65, 54). Although we have a gutshot straight draw to the wheel and an overcard with our Ace, a continuation bet is likely to get called or even raised frequently. Checking behind allows us to control the pot size, realize our equity for free, and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have flopped top pair on a board that is now 4-8-5-6. The turn 6c adds potential straight draws for my opponent, specifically involving cards like 3-7, 7-9, or 3-4. Since my opponent checked behind on the flop, their hand range appears capped, making it unlikely they have a monster hand. I want to build the pot for value while also providing protection against any straight or flush draws. A bet of 300 (roughly 55% of the pot) should extract value from smaller pairs, middle pairs, or straight draws while forcing the opponent to pay to see the river.

The turn brings a 6, placing four connected-ish cards on the board (4-5-6-8), and Flashlight leads out. While a 2 would give me a wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5), this draw is extremely fragile. If Flashlight already holds a 7, he currently has an 8-high straight, meaning I would be drawing dead even if a 2 hits. A 7 would put a straight on the board, giving me a chopped pot at best, but I would easily lose to anyone holding a 9. Holding only Ace-high with at best four clean outs to a bottom-end straight, the pot odds do not justify calling this lead. Folding is the safest and most optimal play.

I am on the button with Jack-Five suited (J5c). While suited cards offer some playability, J5s is quite weak even in a 3-handed game. Opening too wide from the button can lead to difficult post-flop situations against active opponents like Flashlight and Pro. Given the current stack sizes and my desire to maintain a solid image, I will fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening range.

I am in the small blind with Queen-high suited. Since Flash has folded, I am facing the Big Blind. Completing the blind is a standard strategy in this position to see a flop with a speculative hand while controlling the pot size, given that we are playing deep-stacked (around 100BB). I will call the additional 50 to match the 100 total and see if the Big Blind checks.

I hold pocket nines (99) in the Big Blind. The Small Blind has just limped in. 99 is a premium hand in this 3-handed situation, especially against a limp, which typically caps my opponent's range to speculative or marginal hands. I should raise to punish the limp, build the pot for value, and take the betting initiative. A raise to 350 is a strong 3.5x sizing that will either win the pot immediately or extract value while maintaining positional advantage.

I am out of position against Pro who has raised my completion. Qh6h is a marginal hand that plays poorly in a bloated pot out of position. Calling 250 to see a flop with a weak hand against a raise is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

5d3h is a very weak starting hand that offers no value in this spot. Even with positional advantage, it is mathematically correct to fold such a poor hand and wait for a more favorable opportunity to enter the pot.

With 65o in the small blind and facing the big blind out of position, the hand is simply too weak to justify a limp or a raise. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term decision here to preserve my stack.
I am on the Button with a very weak hand (J3o). Being first to act pre-flop, this is an easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 6d, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button has already folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While I only need to put in 50 more chips to see a flop, 26o plays very poorly out of position and has almost no equity. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger hand than to waste chips trying to play a bottom-tier hand out of position.

I am on the button with T4s in a 3-handed game. While ranges are wider in short-handed play, T4s is generally at the bottom of or just outside a standard opening range from the button. Given that the players in the blinds have shown some willingness to contest pots or fold selectively, I will opt for a fold here to maintain a solid image and wait for a more profitable opening candidate.

My hand, 3s-6d, is extremely weak and offers poor playability out of position against the Big Blind. Given the tournament situation, there is no strategic incentive to invest more chips into this pot. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent play to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-Six offsuit. While this is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to attempt to steal the blinds in late position given the passive play of the blinds in previous hands. Raising allows me to take control of the pot pre-flop or potentially play in position. A raise to 250 is a standard sizing to open the pot.
J8 offsuit is too weak to call a raise, especially from the Small Blind where I'll be out of position postflop against the pre-flop raiser. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I have been dealt pocket Aces, the best possible starting hand. Flashlight raised from the button to 250, and the small blind has folded. I need to three-bet here for value and to build a pot. A raise to 850 is a standard size that extracts value and puts pressure on the button's opening range.

I opened K-6 offsuit on the button as a standard blind steal attempt. The big blind (Flash) has re-raised to 850, indicating a strong range that dominates my hand. K-6 offsuit is not strong enough to call a 3-bet, even in position, as it will likely struggle to connect with the board or stay ahead of the opponent's range. Investing an additional 600 chips to continue with such a marginal hand is negative expected value, so the disciplined choice is to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.
98 offsuit is too weak to open raise from the button, even 3-handed. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven of spades. Since the Button has folded, it is down to a blind-vs-blind battle. T7s is well above the average hand in this scenario and has good playability post-flop. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the hand, rather than just limping in.

I am in the big blind with Jack-Seven suited. Flash, the small blind, has raised to 300. With 200 more to call into a pot of 450, the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, which makes calling mathematically sound for this hand type. I have been playing somewhat aggressively, but there is no need to bloat the pot with a marginal hand out of position. I will call and see a flop.

I have a flush draw on a board that strongly favors my pre-flop raising range, as it contains both the Ace and King of spades. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage here. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent a strong hand like an Ace or King while building the pot in case I hit my flush. This bet puts pressure on Flashlight's marginal hands and allows me to maintain the initiative in the hand.

The flop of Ace of Spades, King of Spades, and Eight of Clubs is extremely favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range. My hand, Jack-Seven suited, has missed this board completely, offering no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Continuing against a continuation bet on this texture out of position with no equity is a significant negative-EV play. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal decision to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9c 2c. While suited hands have some potential, 92s is near the bottom of the range and generally too weak to open raise from the button in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack and don't need to force a play with a marginal hand like this. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

With a weak hand like eight-five offsuit in the small blind, playing out of position against the big blind is not profitable. Folding is the most disciplined and effective strategy here to conserve my stack.

Queen-eight offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially in an unraised pot. As the button, while I have position, this hand does not meet the threshold for an opening raise or a call against the blinds. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play to maintain stack health.

T5 suited is a marginal hand out of position in the Small Blind. Given the aggressive tendencies of the Big Blind (who has demonstrated 3-betting and aggressive post-flop play), folding is the most solid and lowest variance play here to preserve chips for a better spot.

4s 8h is a very weak unsuited hand with no high card strength, flush potential, or meaningful straight draw possibilities. From the Button, this is a clear fold pre-flop to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 89o after the Button has folded. This is a standard blind-vs-blind situation. 89o is a decent hand to put pressure on the Big Blind. Raising allows me to potentially take down the pot immediately or play a pot with initiative if called. I will raise to 300 to test the Big Blind's defense.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five offsuit. The Small Blind (Flash) has raised to 300. With 200 more to call into a pot of 400, I have 3:1 pot odds. While K5o is not a premium hand, defending against a Small Blind open is strategically sound to prevent being run over, especially given Flash's aggressive tendencies. Calling allows me to see a flop in position relative to the Small Blind, though I must be cautious if I miss entirely.

I have completely missed this flop with 98o on a monotone 5d 2d 3d board. I have no diamonds, no pair, and no straight draws. Since the board is highly coordinated and favors the Big Blind's calling range, a continuation bet is risky. I will check to see how the opponent reacts and evaluate the situation on the turn, though I am prepared to fold to most bets.

The board is highly coordinated with three diamonds, creating a very dangerous texture for a pair of fives. Since the small blind checked, they could be trapping with a made hand or a diamond draw, or they may simply be yielding the initiative on a board that favors the big blind's defending range. Betting into this board with a marginal pair risks inflating the pot against a better hand or a strong draw while not getting much value from worse hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size and take a free card to see if my pair holds or improves.

The turn is the 2s, pairing the board but not changing the texture significantly. I still have no pair and no draw with 9-high. Flashlight checked back on the flop, which suggests a hand with some showdown value or a weak draw that didn't want to bet into a monotone board. Since I have zero equity and have already shown weakness by checking the flop, checking again is the most prudent move. If the opponent checks behind, I get a free look at the river; if they bet, I can comfortably fold knowing I have no hand to continue with.

The board texture is highly dangerous, featuring three diamonds and a paired deuce, which significantly strengthens the ranges of hands that hit the board (full houses, flushes, or trips). Since the Small Blind checked, I am facing a situation where I have marginal showdown value with my pair of fives. Betting at this point is unlikely to extract value from weaker hands, as most hands that would call are better than mine, and it would only inflate the pot if I am already beaten. Checking behind allows me to maintain pot control and navigate to a showdown, avoiding unnecessary risk against a potential check-trap.

I have improved to top pair on the river after both players checked the flop and the turn. The board is quite coordinated with three diamonds and straight possibilities (A4 or 46), but the passive action from the Big Blind on the flop and turn suggests they likely do not have a flush or a straight. By betting now, I can extract value from weaker hands that might have been checking along, such as Ace-high, or small pocket pairs like 66, 77, or 88. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard value size that can be called by those weaker holdings while not risking too much if the opponent was indeed slow-playing a monster.

Flash's check-check line through the flop and turn suggests a lack of a strong made hand. Their river bet of 300 into a 900 pot is a small sizing, which often represents a polarized range, possibly a missed draw or a thin value bet with a weak pair. My pair of 5s, while marginal, has decent showdown value. Given the pot odds of 4:1, calling is the most profitable play to keep the opponent honest and potentially win the pot against a bluff.

In a 3-handed game, King-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the button. I have the chip lead and have been playing aggressively, which should allow me to take the pot down pre-flop or play effectively in position if called by the blinds. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven suited, which is a strong enough hand to contest the button's opening raise. Since Flash has been active and aggressive, a 3-bet is the optimal play to take initiative, define the hand range, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. A raise to 950 puts sufficient pressure on the opponent and balances my 3-betting range.
Q6 offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, folding is the only reasonable decision to preserve my stack.

I opened K7o from the button, which is a standard steal attempt in a 3-handed game. Flashlight responded with a substantial 3-bet to 950 from the small blind. K7o is not strong enough to call a 3-bet, especially one that is over 3x my opening raise, as it is frequently dominated by an opponent's 3-betting range (AK, KQ, KJ, etc.) or facing a pocket pair. Since I am in the lead and it is still early in the tournament, I will fold and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. I should take the initiative and open the pot to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand. A raise to 250 is consistent with my strategy and effective stack size.

Facing a 2.5x open from the Button. While the Button's range is likely wide, A8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind and leaves me out of position post-flop, while a 3-bet with this specific hand is slightly too loose without a read. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal choice to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. Flashlight opened from the Button, and since the Small Blind folded, I am closing the action. Given Flashlight's frequent opening range, I will 3-bet for value and to take the initiative. A raise to 850 (approx 3.4x the open) is a standard size to put pressure on the Button's wide range and define the hand strength early.

I opened with Ace-Five offsuit on the button, which is a standard play, but facing a significant 3-bet from the big blind indicates strength. Ace-Five offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a 3-betting range, and I do not want to bloat the pot out of position or with a weak holding. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

Q6s is a marginal hand even on the button. While a steal attempt is possible, it's generally safer to fold this hand, especially since both opponents in the blinds have shown a willingness to 3-bet. I'll conserve my stack and look for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. After the Button folds, the action is on me. J4o is a very weak hand and does not perform well post-flop, especially being out of position. While I could attempt a steal, Flashlight has shown some aggression in previous hands (like Hand #21). Given my chip lead, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity rather than playing a marginal hand out of position.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine suited, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I will put in a standard opening raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Three offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Defending the small blind against a button raise with such a marginal holding is mathematically unfavorable and puts me in a difficult spot out of position. It is best to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

We are in the Big Blind facing a standard 3x raise from the Button. Our hand, 5s4s, is a suited connector with good playability post-flop. On a flop of Ts 7h 9c, my 5s 4s completely misses the board aside from a very weak backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser and evaluate their action. I have no strong made hand or immediate draw to justify leading out.

With middle pair and an Ace kicker, my hand has significant showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for multiple streets of value on such a coordinated board (T-9-7-5). After checking back the flop for pot control, checking the turn as well allows me to keep the pot small and ensures I reach the river with a hand that can often win at showdown. Pro's second check suggests they are likely holding a marginal hand or a draw, and I prefer to see a cheap showdown rather than risk being check-raised.

The river brings a King, and we are left with bottom pair (Fives) on a fairly coordinated board. Checking is the most sensible play. We have some marginal showdown value against missed draws or high cards, but not enough to value bet. Turning this into a bluff doesn't make much sense since Flash checking the flop and turn indicates a marginal hand that might still call or a total miss that we already beat. We check to try and get to showdown, and we will fold if Flash decides to bet.

I have middle pair (Nines) with an Ace kicker on a board of 10-7-9-5-K. After Pro checked the flop, turn, and river, it's highly likely they have a marginal hand or a missed draw. My hand has solid showdown value, but there is little to be gained by betting; better hands like a Ten or a King will not fold, and worse hands are unlikely to call a river bet. With Ace-Four offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open with, given the positional advantage. With 9s 8c (offsuit connector) in the Small Blind facing a Button raise, the hand is too weak to play out of position. It lacks the equity and playability needed to defend, so folding is the standard and most profitable decision.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 4d. Flashlight opened from the Button to 250. Holding 7c 5d, a very weak unsuited one-gapper, on the Button. The hand has almost no playability and very poor showdown value. The standard and optimal play is to fold pre-flop to avoid bleeding chips.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair (2h 2c). Since the Button folded, it is down to me and the Big Blind. Pocket pairs, even small ones, have good equity in heads-up situations. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or define Flashlight's range.

We are in the big blind against a raise from the small blind. The pot odds are favorable to defend with King-Six offsuit, as blind-versus-blind ranges are wider. Flash has been aggressive, and calling allows me to see a flop with position and a decent high card, maintaining a reasonable stack-to-blind ratio.

I raised pre-flop with pocket deuces and was called by the Big Blind. I have top pair with a weak kicker (King-Six) on a relatively dry board. Since my opponent checked, taking the free card is the prudent move to control the pot. On the turn (7s), the board remains quite dry (Kh-4d-Tc-7s). I will check again to keep the pot small and see the river for free, or fold if they decide to bet, as my hand is unlikely to be best.

With top pair and a weak kicker on a relatively dry board, betting for value is risky as it likely isolates me against better kings or hands that have me beat. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and reach a showdown cheaply, minimizing losses against better holdings while still retaining showdown value against bluffs.

The river is a 6c, making the final board Kh-4d-Tc-7s-6c. Given that Flashlight has checked back on both the flop and the turn, it is unlikely they hold a King or a Ten. Betting here is unlikely to fold out any pair better than mine (like a 4, 6, or 7), and I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff when I might win the pot by checking. Upon reviewing my hand, I realize I have two pair (Kings and Sixes) on a board of Kh, 4d, Tc, 7s, 6c. My previous assessment was inaccurate in calling it just top pair. Flashlight checked the flop and turn, then bet half-pot on the river. While my pocket deuces are a pair, they are the lowest possible pair on this board (K-T-7-6-4). I am on the button with 8d 7h, a reasonable hand to open in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack and being in position allows me to put pressure on the blinds. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with [Jd, 2d], which is a weak, uncoordinated hand. My hand, Queen-Eight offsuit, is too weak to call a 3x raise out of position against the Button's opening range. Folding is the standard and mathematically sound play here to conserve chips early in the tournament.

My hand is 3d 2s, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite being in the button position, there is no strategic value in playing such a low-equity hand. Folding is the correct professional decision to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. Ace-high hands are quite strong in blind-versus-blind situations. I will raise to 300 to either win the pot immediately or take control of the hand going into the flop.

Defending the big blind with Q9o against a small blind raise is a standard play given the pot odds and position. Folding would be too tight, and while raising is an option, calling is the most balanced approach to control the pot size and see a flop.

I have top pair with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry board (2h-3h-7d). While there is a heart draw, my Seven of Spades gives me a solid hand. I am on the button with T8o. In a three-handed game, this is a standard hand to open-raise with to apply pressure to the blinds and take control of the pot. I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Three offsuit, a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Js 9s is a strong suited connector, making it a highly playable hand on the button. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size to claim the initiative and potentially win the blinds or play the hand in position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit (Ah 8s). Flashlight opened from the button for 300. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a board that contains a King. While I have Ace-high and the Nut Flush backdoor draw (Ah), I do not have a made hand. The flop is relatively dry, and the King is a card that favors my opening range. Since I have the initiative and the opponent checked, a small continuation bet is a standard play to represent strength and potentially take down the pot, as the opponent likely does not have a King given the check.

Flashlight's continuation bet on a King-high board is very standard for a button raiser. On the turn, I am out of position against Flashlight, who was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop. I hold Jack-high with no pair and no significant draw. My opponent called my flop continuation bet, which indicates they likely have some form of strength or a draw. While Ace-high has some showdown value against missed straight draws like 98 or 54, it is unlikely to be the best hand if Flashlight has any pair. Betting as a bluff is an option to try and push him off a weak Queen or a small pocket pair, but since I am the chip leader and the pot is relatively small, I prefer to check and see if he checks behind. If he bets, I will likely fold as Ace-high is a weak bluff-catcher on this texture.

I hold Jack-high on a board of 7h-Kh-6c-Qd-7c. My opponent checked to me on the river. With Queen-high on an Ace-high board and no draw, I have essentially no equity in the pot. Since the pre-flop aggressor (the Button) has yet to act, checking is the standard and correct play to control the pot size and avoid putting more chips into a hand where I am almost certainly behind.

With top pair and an eight kicker on a very dry flop, a continuation bet is standard after both opponents check. I have Jack-high on an Ace-high board (Ah 7c 3s) with no draws and no pieces of the board. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has placed a continuation bet into two players. Given that the Ace is a very strong card for the button's opening range and I have zero equity in this hand, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

The board is Ace-high and extremely dry (Ah-7c-3s). My hand (Q-high) has absolutely no equity against any pair or Ace that the opponent is representing with their continuation bet. I am in the small blind with King-high suited. The button folded, and I only need to call 50 chips to see the flop against the big blind. Flashlight limped from the Small Blind, showing weakness or a speculative hand. With Jack-Queen suited, I have a strong broadway hand that plays very well post-flop. Raising to 350 punishes the limp, takes the betting initiative, and leverages my positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

I am in the small blind with King-high suited (Kh 3h). While K3s is not a premium holding, it is suited and has some playability. The big blind has raised to 350, which is a standard steal attempt. The flop of 2h 5h Qc gives me a King-high flush draw. As the out-of-position player who called the pre-flop raise, checking is the standard approach to control the pot size. Folding would be too passive given the strength of my draw, and raising as a semi-bluff is unnecessary when I can simply call and evaluate the turn. I will call to realize my equity.

I am in the small blind, acting first on the turn. I have a King-high flush draw on a board of 2h 5h Qc Ac. My opponent, the big blind, has shown consistent aggression pre-flop and on the flop. The turn Ace downgraded my top pair to second pair, and after I checked behind, Flashlight leads out for half pot on the river. My hand, 6s Tc, is weak and uncoordinated. Even playing from the button in a three-handed game, this hand has poor playability and is dominated by most calling ranges. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the optimal long-term strategy.

King-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand to play from the Small Blind. Being out of position against the Big Blind (who is currently the chip leader) makes it difficult to play post-flop. Folding is a safe, low-variance decision that preserves our stack for better, more profitable spots.

9s 5s is a weak suited gapper. Even from the Button in a 3-handed game, this hand is generally too weak to open profitably, especially against opponents who have shown aggression. Folding to preserve my stack and waiting for better starting hands is the most solid play.

I am on the button with T9o, which is a standard opening hand in a 3-handed game. I have a healthy chip lead and position on both opponents. A standard 3x raise is consistent with my previous play and should allow me to take control of the pot early or play a manageable pot in position if called.

K2 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Facing a 3x open from the Button, folding is the standard and optimal decision to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 7c. Flashlight raised to 3x from the Button and Pro folded. I am getting 2.25:1 odds to call (200 more into a pot of 450). 9c 7c is a solid speculative hand that plays well post-flop, especially in a heads-up pot where I close the action. I will call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind and have hit second pair (Sevens) on an Ace-high flop (5c Ah 7d). Flashlight, who raised pre-flop from the button, is the aggressor. I hold top pair, top kicker (AdKd) on an A-high dry board. Since I am in position and the opponent checked, this is a clear value bet situation. Betting roughly half-pot keeps the pot growing while getting value from weaker Aces or pairs. Checking back would only offer a free card and lose value.

I have improved to two pair (Nines and Sevens) on the turn. The board is Ah-7d-5c-9d. I hold top pair, top kicker (Ad Kd) and the nut flush draw on the turn. Since the opponent checked, a bet is the best way to extract value from worse Aces and pairs, while continuing to build the pot. Checking back would be too passive and miss value. I have improved to two pair (Nines and Sevens) on the turn. While the Ace on the board is a scare card, my hand is now ahead of Flashlight's likely range of single-pair Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, etc.) which he has been representing with his pre-flop raise and continuation bets on the flop and turn. Since he has shown consistent aggression, I will check-raise for value. A raise to 1600 (adding 1000 on top of his 600 bet) targets his Ax hands and draws like diamonds or straights (though 86 has arrived, I must still bet for value against his wider range).

However, I hold top pair with the nut flush draw (Ad), giving me substantial equity against a significant portion of their value range. Folding would be abandoning a high-equity hand, and raising risks bloating the pot against a range that potentially has me crushed. On a board of 5c-Ah-7d-9d-7s, I have a full house (Sevens full of Nines). Flashlight has shown strength throughout the hand, raising pre-flop, betting the flop, and betting the turn. My full house beats all trip combinations. I will lead for value, betting 2200 into the 4450 pot, aiming to get called by any Ace-x hand in Flashlight's range.

I hold top pair, top kicker (Ace-King) on a board that missed the potential flush draw. The opponent check-raised the turn, suggesting they could have a value hand like trips or two-pair, but they could also be turning a missed draw (like a diamond draw) into a bluff. Raising would isolate me against hands that have me beat, while calling allows me to see the showdown profitably.
Q3o is a very weak, unsuited starting hand. Even from the Button, it is not strong enough to open-raise or call, making it an easy fold pre-flop to conserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-5 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded. While it's only 50 more to call, this hand is very weak and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Against Flashlight in the Big Blind, who has a medium stack, there is no need to commit chips with a sub-par hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have trip sixes (6s, 6d, 6h) on a board that, while coordinated with straight and flush possibilities (6s 9d 7s 6d Ts), remains a very strong hand in this spot. I am on the button with Ten-Six suited in a three-handed game. With a significant stack lead over both opponents, I should maintain pressure from the button. T6s is a reasonable hand to open for a raise in this position to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position against the shorter stacks.

Holding Jack-Deuce offsuit in the small blind against an aggressive button open is a clear fold. With Ad3d in the Big Blind facing a Button open, I have a strong enough hand to defend. Suited Aces play well post-flop, offering opportunities to hit nut flush draws, wheel straight draws, or top pair. Given the pot odds, calling is a standard and profitable play.

I have flopped three of a kind (sixes) with a Ten kicker. The board is paired and contains a flush draw (spades). As the pre-flop aggressor, I want to lead out for value and protection against draws. Facing a continuation bet on an 8s-6s-6d board, my Ad3d has very little playability. I only have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw, and I am out of position. I hold a pocket pair of sixes in the button position. This is a strong starting hand in a three-player game. Raising is the optimal play to take initiative, define the range of my opponents, and leverage my positional advantage.

K5 suited is too weak to call a 3x raise out of position from the small blind, especially with the big blind still left to act. A 3-bet bluff is possible but not strictly necessary here, so folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve my stack.

My pocket pair of sixes is currently an underpair to the Queen, Ten, and Eight on the board. My opponent, the Big Blind, checked the flop, which typically indicates a weaker range or a passive play. My pocket pair of sixes is an underpair on a board of Ten-Eight-Four-Queen-Four. Flash checked the flop, then led out on the turn and river, showing strength. Holding offsuit connectors (8s 7h) in the Big Blind against a 3x open from the Button is generally a fold, as it lacks both high-card strength and playability when out of position. Considering our stack size and positional disadvantage, defending this hand is not profitable.

The hand 2s6s is extremely weak and does not warrant a call, especially when playing at a competitive level in a 50/100 structure. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my chip stack for more favorable future opportunities.

With J9o in the small blind against the big stack, folding is the most prudent play. J9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, and engaging the active chip leader without a strong holding is an unnecessary risk.

Holding A8o on the button in a 3-handed game is a clear open. An ace-high hand has solid equity, and raising allows us to potentially steal the blinds right here or play in position post-flop if called. A standard 3x raise to 300 puts appropriate pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven of Hearts, a decent suited semi-connector. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Pro is the short stack with only 4,250 chips. I am in the big blind with 9s-4c, a very weak hand. Even with favorable pot odds to call (200 to win 700), this hand has poor playability in a three-way pot and is out of position. It is best to conserve my stack and fold.

The flop is Kd 2d Ad, giving us zero direct interaction. I have no diamonds, and my Ten-high has very little showdown value on this board. On this highly coordinated, monotone flop (Kd 2d Ad), we have top pair but a weak kicker and no diamond. The turn card (4s) doesn't improve my hand, which is currently Ten-high with no diamonds on a monotone King-Ace-Deuce board. By checking behind again, we maintain pot control, safely navigate to the river, and induce potential bluffs from missed draws or worse hands on the final street.

The board is extremely unfavorable for my Ten-high (Kd Ad 2d 4s 3c). I have no diamonds and no piece of this board. With the board showing three diamonds and four to a straight (A-2-3-4), the runout is very dangerous. I am on the button with 9c7c, a decent suited semi-connector. My stack is very large compared to the blinds and my opponents' remaining stacks. While 3-betting could work as a bluff, it commits a significant portion of our 30 BB stack and struggles if called. Calling allows me to play in position against a relatively wide SB opening range while keeping the pot size manageable.

The flop is extremely wet with three diamonds and a high Queen. Since I do not hold a diamond in my hand (7h 7s), my pocket sevens are very vulnerable and provide almost no equity against a flush or a better made hand. Checking is the most prudent strategy here to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the opponent's actions, rather than committing more chips to a dangerous board.

On a monotone diamond flop, holding the bare Ace of Diamonds gives me the nut flush draw. Flashlight checking to me presents a good opportunity to semi-bluff. Betting small on a monotone board is standard because it pressures hands without a diamond while risking fewer chips. If called, I have plenty of equity to hit the flush or an Ace on later streets, and I can continue to barrel favorably on many turn cards.

The opponent has made a small bet of 200 into an 800 pot, offering me 4:1 pot odds. While the board is dangerous with three diamonds and I lack a diamond, folding pocket sevens to such a small bet is mathematically incorrect given the pot odds. Calling allows me to see the turn card relatively cheaply to determine if I can improve my hand or if the betting action indicates I am drawing dead.

The board has become extremely dangerous with a pair of Queens and three diamonds. My pocket sevens are effectively a bluff catcher and have no equity against a made flush or any Queen. As I am out of position, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and re-evaluate if the opponent bets.

With the board pairing on the turn, my flush draw loses some value against potential full houses or trips, and betting could walk right into a check-raise from a Queen. Taking a free card with the nut flush draw and an overcard is the most prudent play, allowing me to realize my equity while keeping the pot size manageable.

The board has become extremely dangerous with four diamonds. My pocket sevens have no showdown value against any diamond, any Queen, or any other pair that the opponent might have in their range. Betting would only isolate me against hands that have me beat. Checking is the only logical action to potentially reach a cheap showdown and minimize losses.

The river brings the fourth diamond, giving me the nut flush with my Ace of Diamonds. Flashlight checking for a third time strongly suggests they don't have a full house or quads, and are likely sitting on a worse flush, a Queen, or a marginal pair. To extract maximum value from hands like the King or Jack of diamonds, or a skeptical Queen, I should put out a value bet. A bet of around 60% pot is ideal to get called by these weaker made hands while not risking my entire stack.

The board is extremely dangerous with four diamonds and a pair of Queens, giving any player holding a single diamond a flush. My pocket sevens only give me two pair (Queens and Sevens), which is highly vulnerable. Given that the opponent is betting 600 into a 1000 pot on the river after the fourth diamond hit, they are likely representing the flush or a better Queen. Folding is the only sensible decision, as I am almost certainly beaten.

I am on the button with pocket fours, which is a strong enough hand to open from this position. With 42 big blinds, a standard raise to 300 is appropriate to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds or gain positional advantage post-flop. Given the stack depths, raising is superior to limping.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 9h, a weak offsuit connector. Facing a raise from the Button and being out of position, this hand lacks the equity and playability to call or 3-bet profitably. Folding is the standard and best option to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Four suited. Flashlight, who is on the Button with a relatively short stack of 3,900 chips, has raised to 300. The Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a total pot of 450. The pot odds are approximately 2.25:1, meaning I need about 31% equity to justify a call. Queen-Four suited typically has enough equity against a standard Button opening range to see a flop, especially since I have the advantage of a large stack and the potential to realize equity with a suited hand. A raise is unnecessary and risky against a short stack that might jam, so I will call and evaluate the flop.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Queens, 4 kicker) on a somewhat coordinated board (Q-J-3 with two clubs). Flashlight was the pre-flop aggressor from the button and has a relatively short stack of 3,900. Standard play here is to check to the raiser to see how they react. If they bet, I have a clear call given the strength of my pair against a wide button opening range, but I want to avoid bloating the pot immediately with a weak kicker.

The board features a Queen and a Jack, which are high cards that hit the Big Blind's calling range significantly harder than my pocket fours. Betting here is likely to isolate me against hands that already have me beaten (any Queen, Jack, or better pocket pair) or flush draws. Checking back allows me to keep the pot small, evaluate the turn, and potentially realize my equity without inflating the pot against a likely stronger range.

On the turn, the 6d is a complete blank. After checking the flop and seeing Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) check back, their range is significantly capped. It is unlikely they are holding a monster like AA, KK, AQ, or QJ, as those would typically continuation bet on a board with multiple draws (clubs and straight possibilities). They likely hold a Jack, a medium pocket pair, or a draw. Since I have top pair (Queens), I am very likely ahead. I will bet 400 into the 650 pot to extract value from Jx hands and charge any draws (like KT or flush draws) for the river.

The opponent, Flash, led out with a 400 chip bet on the turn after checking the flop. My hand, pocket fours, has very little showdown value on this board of Queen, Jack, Three, and Six. With two overcards on the board, it is highly likely that the opponent's range is stronger than mine. Calling would require me to likely fold the river on most runouts unless I improve. Given the stack depth and the board texture, folding is the most disciplined and prudent strategy to preserve my stack.

K4o is a very weak hand, even when first to act on the button. With around 35 big blinds, there's no need to force a steal with such a marginal holding, especially with the large stack in the small blind who might play back aggressively. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Although I have a significant chip lead and have been successfully bullying the table, 8-4 offsuit is near the bottom of my range. Flashlight is a relatively short stack compared to me but still has about 38 big blinds, meaning I don't want to gift him chips by playing trash hands out of position. Folding here is the standard and most disciplined play.

I am on the button with J7 offsuit. While I have a massive chip lead (22,500 compared to ~3,500-4,000 for my opponents), J7o is a relatively weak hand to open with in this spot. Both Flashlight and Pro are short-stacked enough to be looking for shove opportunities or to defend their blinds aggressively. I prefer to wait for a stronger hand to apply pressure or to engage in pots where I have better post-flop equity, especially since I want to avoid doubling up the shorter stacks unnecessarily.

I am in the Small Blind after the button has folded. With an Ace in my hand (A3o), this is a favorable spot to raise and put pressure on the Big Blind. Raising is a standard aggressive move to try and take down the pot pre-flop or take initiative in a heads-up situation.

With pocket Tens in the Big Blind facing a min-raise from the Small Blind, we have a very strong hand for a blind-vs-blind encounter. A 3-bet is definitely the optimal play to extract value, take control of the hand, and potentially deny equity from overcards. I will size up to 700 to build the pot and punish a potentially wide Small Blind opening range.

After opening the Small Blind with A3o, the Big Blind (Pro) responded with a 3-bet to 700. A3o is a weak hand to continue with out of position against a 3-bet, as I will likely be dominated by better Aces or find myself in a difficult post-flop spot. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the button position with 7h-5s, which is a very weak hand with little potential. Given the 3-handed nature of the game and the low strength of the cards, folding is the most disciplined and profitable decision to preserve my stack.

With King-Seven offsuit in the Small Blind, we have a marginal holding. The Big Blind is the massive chip leader and can put significant pressure on us if we choose to play this hand out of position. With 37 big blinds, it's safer to just fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Q9o is a marginal hand to open from the Button 3-handed. Given that the Small Blind (Flash) has a massive chip lead and can pressure us aggressively, and our stack is around 37 BBs, it is safer to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

I am the massive chip leader with 22,500 chips, while both opponents are under 4,000. In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds, Ace-Eight offsuit is a very strong hand. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind (Flashlight), consistent with my aggressive opening strategy throughout the tournament.

Flash is raising from the small blind. Holding Ace-Five suited in the big blind, I am getting sufficient pot odds to defend my blind. Calling keeps the pot size manageable against a large-stacked opponent while allowing me to realize my equity in a heads-up pot. This is a standard defensive play.

On a board of K-9-4, I have Ace-high with one overcard. As the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-vs-blind battle, this is a standard board to continuation bet. The King is very much in my perceived range, and a half-pot bet puts pressure on the shorter stack (Flashlight) to have a piece of the board or a draw to continue. If I am raised, I can easily fold, but a bet allows me to take down the pot immediately if they missed.

I hold the nut flush draw (Ace of clubs and Five of clubs) on a board with two clubs. The opponent (Flash) has bet 300 into a 900 pot, giving me good pot odds to call. With significant equity against a range that includes top pair or middle pair, calling is the optimal play to realize my equity and potentially hit the flush on future streets.

The turn card (6s) is largely a blank. Having been called on the flop, I have to consider that Flashlight might have a King, a Nine, or a draw. My Ace-high has some showdown value but very little equity if called by a better hand. Since I have a massive stack advantage, there is no need to overplay this hand or turn it into a multi-street bluff against a short stack who has already shown interest in the pot. Checking allows me to control the pot and potentially see a cheap showdown or fold if they bet significantly.

I am in the big blind with the nut flush draw (Ace of clubs, Five of clubs) on a board of 4h-Kc-9c-6s. The small blind (Flash) has checked to me. Given that I have a significant draw and my opponent checked, checking behind is the optimal play. This allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit my flush on the river, or take the pot down if I hit. Betting here might be risky if Flash is trapping or has a made hand, so taking the free card is the safest and most strategic approach.

On this board of K-9-4-6-T, my Ace-high has very little showdown value, but it is also difficult to turn into a profitable bluff against a short-stacked opponent who called a flop bet. The Ten of Clubs on the river completes some draws and adds complexity to the board. Having checked the turn and seen Flashlight check back, I will check again on the river. This allows me to see a cheap showdown if they check back with a hand like a weaker Ace or a missed draw, and it prevents me from losing additional chips to a better hand or a King they were slow-playing.

I have the nut flush with Ace-high (Ac-5c) on a board of 4h-Kc-9c-6s-Tc. There are no pairs on the board, and no straight flush possibilities, making my hand the absolute nuts. Flash has checked to me on the river. Checking behind would be a significant mistake as I have the best possible hand. I must place a value bet to maximize my earnings. A bet of 750 into the 1200 pot is a solid sizing to induce a call from a King or a weaker flush.

On a board of K-9-4-6-T, my Ace-high has virtually no showdown value against an opponent who has called a flop bet and then bets 750 on the river after I checked twice. Since I lose to any pair (K, 9, 4, 6, or T) and all completed draws, I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am on the button with Queen-Deuce offsuit. I am in the small blind with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a strong starting hand for a heads-up situation against the big blind. The board of King-Jack-Jack is very disconnected but heavily favors the big blind's calling range, as they are more likely to have King or Jack combinations than I am. On this dry, paired board (Kc Jd Js), I have top pair with a weak kicker (Ks 6s). This is a classic 'way ahead or way behind' situation. The board is paired with two Jacks, making trips or a full house possible, though less likely given the lack of aggression from my opponent. Since the opponent checked both the flop and the turn, they are likely weak or trapping. Betting now would likely only force out hands I am beating while getting called by better hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size and take a free card to the river.

The opponent has checked twice, signaling weakness, likely a missed draw, a lower pocket pair, or a give-up. With top pair (Kings and Jacks), we likely have the best hand. Betting here protects our hand against diamond flush draws and straight draws that picked up equity on the 9d turn, while extracting value from weaker holdings that might call a reasonable sizing.

The opponent has checked the flop and then made a 300 bet into a 900 pot on the turn. My pair of 9s has some showdown value, and with 4:1 pot odds, calling is the most profitable play. Raising would likely force out hands I am beating while only getting called by better hands, so I will stick with a call to see the river.

The board is K-K-J-J-9. My two-pair hand (KKJJ with an Ace kicker) is potentially strong but very vulnerable, as any King or Jack in the opponent's hand gives them a full house. Betting here is risky because it only gets called by better hands (full houses) and folds out the bluffs I am currently beating. Checking allows me to reach showdown safely and potentially see a check-down if the opponent also has a marginal hand.

The river King improves my hand to Kings full of Jacks, which is an absolute monster. The only hand that beats me is exactly quad Jacks (Jc Jh). If my opponent holds any Jack, they now have Jacks full of Kings and will almost certainly pay off a large value bet, potentially even raising. If they hold a hand like AA, QQ, or a 9, a well-sized bet might still induce a hero call. I will bet heavily to extract maximum value from a Jack or any strong bluff-catchers.

The board has paired Kings and Jacks, creating a very strong board texture where a full house is highly likely for anyone holding a King or a Jack. My hand, King-King-Jack-Jack-Ace, is a Two Pair hand that loses to any King or Jack in the opponent's range. While checking the flop might have indicated a weaker hand or a trap, the opponent's betting aggression on the turn and the significant river bet strongly suggest they have a made hand (a King or a Jack). Given the strength of the board, my hand is essentially a bluff catcher that loses to the majority of the opponent's value range, and calling here is unprofitable.

KJs is a very strong starting hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Raising from the button is the standard move to seize initiative and build the pot. A raise to 300 is consistent with my typical opening size.

I am dealt 5c 3h in the Small Blind, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button, the most mathematically sound and straightforward decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

With 10-4 suited on the Button and 42.5 BBs, my hand is simply too weak to open. Although it is suited, it lacks straight potential and high card strength, making it easily dominated. The standard and most profitable play is to fold.

The flop of King-Deuce-Trey with two spades does not connect with my Six-Seven offsuit at all. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no meaningful straight draw. I am on the button with a massive chip lead over both opponents. Jack-Five suited is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game from this position. I hold pocket Aces, the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am in the Small Blind and facing a raise from the Button, who has been an aggressive player throughout the session. A 3-bet is the optimal strategy to build the pot, take control of the hand, and narrow the field against an active opponent. I will raise to 1000 to maximize value while maintaining the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind holding 3s 2h, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, there is absolutely no reason to get involved. Folding is the only viable option.

I am holding 9h-6d, which is a weak, unsuited connector with poor playability. Being in the button position, there is no strategic advantage to playing such a hand when I can simply fold and wait for a better opportunity. My stack of 4200 is sufficient to remain patient.

K6o is a marginal hand from the Small Blind. Flash is the massive chip leader and will likely defend wide or 3-bet aggressively. Folding to conserve chips and wait for a better spot is the safest and most optimal play here.

T3o is a very weak starting hand, far outside any standard opening range from the button. With 41 big blinds left, there is no reason to get involved with trash hands. Folding is the only optimal play.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 6d. I am in the big blind with 5c 4s, which is a weak, unsuited connector. I am on the button with a massive chip lead over both opponents who are sitting on roughly 40 big blinds each. In a three-handed game, I should be opening a wide range from the button to apply pressure. Although Jack-Six offsuit is a marginal hand, my position and stack advantage allow me to open and potentially take the blinds or outplay them post-flop. I will put in a standard raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven suited. This is a very playable hand, especially in a blind defense situation against the button. Given Flash's large stack, I should see a flop to evaluate, as I have decent equity and position relative to the button (if I were big blind, but here I am SB). I am dealt 74o in the Big Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, my hand has very poor equity and zero playability post-flop. The clear decision is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind and checked the flop with bottom pair (7s) and an Ace kicker. I am out of position against the button, who raised pre-flop. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot size manageable and evaluate my opponent's aggression on this dry board.

On this dry flop of 5s-2c-7h, my Jack-high hand has no direct value, but as the pre-flop aggressor on the button, I have a range advantage. This board is unlikely to have improved a standard calling range from the small blind, and my stack advantage allows me to apply pressure consistently.

I hold middle pair (sevens) on a relatively dry, low-card board (5s-2c-7h). The opponent (Flash) has bet 350 into a pot of 650. With direct pot odds of nearly 3:1, calling is a mathematically sound decision. Raising is unnecessary as it would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and likely only get called by hands that beat me or force folds from hands I currently dominate (like air or underpairs). Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold middle pair with an Ace kicker on a 5-2-7-8 board. The 8d turn is relatively safe, although it opens up straight draw possibilities like 6-9. Since I am out of position, checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and see how my opponent reacts. If they bet, I can re-evaluate, but leading into them with a marginal pair would likely only get called by better hands or force out hands that I am currently ahead of.

On the turn, the 8d gives me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 4 or 9). Flashlight checked for a second time after calling my flop continuation bet. While a second barrel could potentially force a fold from some weak pairs or Ace-high hands, Flashlight's flop call indicates they likely have a piece of this low board. By checking back, I can see the river for free and potentially realize my equity if I hit my straight, while also keeping the pot manageable against a shorter-stacked opponent.

I have a pair of sevens on a board of 5-2-7-8-K. The opponent checked back on the turn, which likely suggests they don't have a strong King or a set/two-pair, though they could be trapping or have a medium-strength hand. My hand has showdown value against potential missed draws or pure bluffs, but betting for value is thin and unlikely to be profitable as most hands that call will beat me. Checking is the best line to get to showdown safely.

On the river, the King of Clubs is a significant scare card that could have hit my range as the pre-flop aggressor. However, my Jack-high has virtually no showdown value, and Flashlight's check on the river follows a check-call on the flop and a check on the turn. Given my massive chip lead and the relatively small size of the pot, I will check back to minimize unnecessary variance and see the showdown, as I am unlikely to force a fold from a medium-strength hand that has already committed chips.

Jc 4d is a weak, unsuited hand with poor connectivity. There is no strategic value in calling or raising with this holding, especially when playing against aggressive opponents like Pro and Flash. Folding is the standard, disciplined play.
98o is a marginal offsuit connector. Being out of position against the large-stacked Big Blind, it's best to fold and preserve my 40 BB stack rather than limp or raise and risk facing aggression.

I have Ace-King suited (Ks As) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. Being the first to act, I want to open with a solid raise to build the pot and thin the field. I am holding 4c 2h in the small blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Pro, the button, has raised to 300. I am holding 9-4 off-suit in the Big Blind after the button opened. This is an extremely weak hand with very little potential to improve or win at showdown. I will fold and conserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 9c 3s, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have a significant chip lead over my opponents, there is no strategic reason to open with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my stack advantage.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (6d 2d) after the button has folded. While I only need to call 50 chips to see a flop, 6d 2d is at the very bottom of my playing range. Folding preserves my stack of 4700 chips without engaging in a marginal situation against the big blind.

K-2 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, even in a blind-versus-button situation. There is no strategic value in investing chips with such a dominated hand, so folding is the optimal decision to preserve the stack.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand, 8c 5s. The Button has folded, and I am facing the Big Blind who has a massive chip lead. Given my weak holding and positional disadvantage, the standard and most profitable play is to fold and wait for a better spot to risk my chips.
Pre-flop on the button with an extremely weak hand (9-3 offsuit). Folding is the only viable option.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand. Since the action folded to me in the small blind, I will put in a standard raise to 300. This size is consistent with my previous pre-flop raises and aims to build value immediately against Flashlight in the big blind.

I hold Ace-Jack suited in the big blind against an aggressive small blind raiser. Given the strength of my hand and the opponent's wide raising frequency throughout this tournament, a 3-bet is the optimal play to assert control, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or build a larger pot with a significant equity advantage. I will raise to 900 to put pressure on Flash.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces (As Ah), the best possible starting hand. After my initial raise to 300, Flashlight has 3-bet to 900. Flashlight's total stack is relatively short at 3,800 chips.
