This allows me to control the pot size and see how the aggressor reacts, as betting into two opponents with a weak pair and poor kicker is unlikely to be profitable.

With Ace-Queen on a 4s 9h Th flop, I have two overcards and a backdoor nut flush draw. However, this board texture is quite wet and heavily favors the calling ranges of the blinds, who could easily have straight draws, flush draws, or pairs. Betting into two opponents here is risky as I'm likely to get called or raised by better hands or strong draws. Checking behind for pot control and a free card is the safest option to realize my equity without bloating the pot.

The turn brings a King of Spades, creating a board with multiple straight and flush draws (4s-9h-Th-Ks). I still have only Ace-high with no draw. Given that there are three players in the pot and the board is highly coordinated, it is unlikely that Ace-high is currently the best hand, and it is certainly not strong enough to bet into two opponents. With a comfortable chip lead and only 10 hands remaining, my priority is to avoid unnecessary risks. I will check to see how the other players react to this overcard.

I am holding a pair of nines with a flush draw on a board of 4s-9h-Th-Ks. Having checked the flop, the turn brings the King of Spades, giving me a spade flush draw. Checking is the most strategic play here, as it allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially realize my equity on the river if a spade hits, without committing further chips into a potentially stronger range from my opponents.

Both opponents have checked again on the turn. The King on the turn gives me a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway straight, and it's a card that fits my pre-flop raising range well. However, the board remains very coordinated with two flush draws (hearts and spades) and multiple straight possibilities. If I bet, I'm mostly getting called by hands that currently beat me or strong draws, and a check-raise would force me to fold my gutshot equity. Taking a free card allows me to fully realize my equity without risking chips when I only have Ace-high.

The board is highly coordinated with potential straights (any Queen) and flushes (three spades on board). My Ace-high has almost no showdown value and definitely cannot call a bet. Since I am first to act and have a significant chip lead with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament, I will check. My goal is to minimize risk and protect my stack.

I have realized that my hand, consisting of the 3s and 9s, combined with the board of 4s, 9h, Th, Ks, Js, actually gives me a King-high flush. Despite my previous thoughts focusing on the pair of nines, the river completion makes my hand significantly stronger. Everyone has checked to me, and with a flush, I should definitely bet for value. Although there is a possibility an opponent holds the Ace of spades for a higher flush, it is unlikely given the checked-down action on all streets. Betting 600 into the 900 pot is a solid value bet that can be called by weaker hands like kings or jacks, or even straight draws that missed.

I river the Broadway straight with my Ace-Queen on a board of 4s 9h Th Ks Js. While this is an incredibly strong hand, there are three spades on the board, meaning a runner-runner flush is possible. My opponent's lead of 600 into a pot of 900 could easily be another Queen looking to chop or value bet, or it could be a backdoor flush that got there. Since I hold the Qc, it reduces some combos of opposing straights. Raising here is risky because worse hands are likely to fold, while hands that beat me (flushes) will call or jam. Calling is the most prudent action to guarantee I get to showdown without value-owning myself against a flush.

On a board of 4s-9h-Th-Ks-Js, any Queen makes a straight and three spades are present for a potential flush. I have only Ace-high (Ac 6d) with no showdown value. Flashlight has bet 600 into two opponents and Pro has called. Given the coordination of the board and the action from both opponents, my hand is definitely beaten. With only 10 hands remaining and a substantial chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips on a hopeless call. I will fold.

I have King-Ten offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a strong starting hand and a standard opening raise. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot while playing in position.

6-8 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against an aggressive button raiser. With the tournament nearing its end and a comfortable stack of 10,600, I have no reason to play a marginal hand. Preserving my stack for a better spot is the disciplined choice.

Holding J5o in the big blind against a button open is a clear fold. The hand is too weak, offsuit, and lacks playability out of position. With 27 BBs remaining, it is best to preserve my stack and look for a more profitable spot to either defend or shove in the remaining hands.

7-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very little potential for improvement or value. Despite being on the button, which is a position of advantage, there is no strategic justification to play such a poor hand against opponents. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with J4o, which is a very weak starting hand. Even though Flashlight folded and I only have Flash left to act against, playing out of position with a marginal holding against the big stack is not profitable. The best decision is to fold and preserve my chips.

On the button with A8o and 26.5 big blinds, I have a standard opening hand. I will raise to 2.5x to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. It's not strong enough to shove, and I don't want to just call and invite the blinds to squeeze or see a free flop. Raising to 250 is consistent with my standard open size.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit (A3o). The Button (Pro), who is the short stack with only 2,400 chips, has raised to 250. I need to call 200 more into a pot that currently has 400 chips. The pot odds are favorable (3:1), and A3o is a reasonable hand to play in a three-handed game, even out of position. As the chip leader with only 8 hands remaining in the tournament, calling allows me to see a flop and potentially eliminate the short stack if I hit, while maintaining my lead without taking the higher risk of a 3-bet against a potentially strong range.
I am in the big blind and the pot odds are attractive (150 to call into a 600 pot). Closing the action, calling with Kd7d is the standard play to see a flop.

I have flopped the nut straight (the wheel). With the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) sitting on a short stack and yet to act, checking is the most strategic move to encourage a continuation bet or a lead from the Big Blind. My goal is to maximize value from the short stack or induce a bluff/draw-heavy bet from Flashlight. If a bet occurs, I intend to check-raise to build the pot and protect my hand against the spade flush draw.

The flop is 5d-2s-4s. I hold Kd-7d, giving me King-high and a backdoor flush draw. This is a very dry hand against two opponents in a multi-way pot. Checking is the standard play, as betting would be an overplay of a hand with very little equity. I will look to fold if there is significant action, or potentially see a turn card if the betting remains small.

With Ad 8h on a 5d 2s 4s flop, I have a gutshot straight draw and an overcard. Although my opponents checked to me, this board connects heavily with the calling ranges of the blinds, who often defend with suited connectors, one-gappers, and small pairs. Given my short stack of 24 big blinds, a continuation bet here into two players is risky, as I'll often get called by draws or better high-card hands, or face a check-raise that forces me off my equity. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card, hoping to hit a 3 for the wheel or an Ace.

I hold a straight (the wheel) with the nut flush blocker (Ace of Spades). Having checked the flop to trap the short-stacked pre-flop aggressor (Pro), I will check the turn as well. The Ten of Spades completes a potential flush, but my A-spade blocker means no one can have the nut flush, and I have a redraw to it if the river is a spade. Checking encourages a bluff or a bet from a second-best hand like a pair of Tens or a lower flush, allowing me to maximize value or potentially eliminate the short stack while maintaining a deceptive line.

I am in the big blind with King-high and no real draws on a board of 5d-2s-4s-Ts. With two opponents, including the original raiser who checked the flop, a bluff here would be high risk and likely ineffective. Checking is the most prudent action to keep the pot small and see the river.

With Ad 8h on a 5d 2s 4s Ts board, the flush has completed and I don't hold a spade. Betting into two players here as a pure bluff is very risky, especially with my limited stack. Both opponents have checked again, but their calling ranges can easily contain a single spade or a pair that won't fold to a single bet. By checking, I take a free river card to potentially hit my gutshot straight (a non-spade 3) or an Ace, while controlling the size of the pot and preserving my chips.

I have a straight (the wheel) on a board that has now paired the Ten. While a flush or a full house is technically possible, the action has been extremely passive with everyone checking the flop and the turn. This suggests that my opponents likely do not have strong made hands like flushes or sets that improved. With the board pairing on the river, I want to extract value from any Ten (trips) or smaller pairs that might have stayed in. Since I am first to act and the pot is small (750), a bet of 450 (60% pot) is a good size to get called by weaker made hands or induced curiosity calls, while also protecting against being outdrawn by a lucky rivered full house if I were to check and let them see it for free (though the hand is over). Given the tournament stage and my chip lead, I'll go for value here.

The board shows 5d-2s-4s-Ts-Th, and I hold Kd-7d. I have King-high with no pair, and the betting sequence was checked down until the river. Flash is betting 450 into a pot of 1200, which suggests they might have a pair (like a Ten or a pocket pair) or are attempting to take down the pot with a bet after checks. Given that I have no showdown value and my hand loses to any pair, calling is mathematically and strategically incorrect. Folding is the only rational decision here.

I have Ace-high on a board with a paired Ten and three spades. My opponent bet 450 on the river after everyone checked the flop and turn. I have no pair, no flush, and my Ace-high is essentially a very weak bluff-catcher. Calling here would unnecessarily risk a significant portion of my already short stack with a hand that loses to any pair. Folding is the most sensible option to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with pocket nines (9h 9s), which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. My goal is to maximize value and take control of the pot. Pro is currently short-stacked with only 2300 chips (23 BB), making them likely to look for a spot to shove. A standard raise to 300 has been my consistent opening size and is appropriate here to build the pot while remaining folded to a significant 3-bet if necessary, though I would likely call a shove from the short stack.

Holding Ace-high in the small blind against a button open is a standard defensive play. The opponent, Flash, has been aggressive throughout the tournament, so defending with an Ace is appropriate. Calling allows me to see a flop while keeping the pot manageable, given the positional disadvantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 5d, which is an extremely weak offsuit gapper. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, I have a clear fold. I am short-stacked with 23 BBs and cannot afford to get involved in a multi-way pot out of position with such a poor hand.

I am out of position with Ace-high on a King-high board. There are no immediate draws for my hand. Checking is the standard play here to see how my opponent proceeds. If they bet, I will likely re-evaluate, but for now, I should not lead into the aggressor.

I have pocket nines on a board with two overcards (King and Ten). While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board connects well with a typical small blind calling range which often includes broadway cards and suited kings. By checking back, I control the pot size and allow myself to see a turn card without risking a check-raise. My hand still has significant showdown value against smaller pairs or missed draws, and checking keeps the pot manageable with a marginal made hand.

I am in the small blind holding Ace-high on a board of K-4-T-2. My opponent (Flash, on the button) checked the flop, indicating weakness. Checking again on the turn provides a good opportunity to take the lead with a bet. Since I have no showdown value, betting is the best way to contest the pot before the river. I will bet 400 into the 700 pot to represent a King or a strong draw, which should be enough to force a fold from my opponent given their previous passivity.

I hold pocket nines on a board of K-4-T-2. After checking back the flop, Flashlight has led for 400 into a 700 pot. My flop check-back was for pot control, and my hand now functions as a bluff-catcher. Flashlight's range includes many Kx and Tx hands that I lose to, but it also includes straight draws (QJ), flush draws (AcXc), and smaller pocket pairs (88-55) that might be betting for protection or as a probe. Given the pot odds (roughly 2.75 to 1), I only need about 27% equity to justify a call. Pocket nines are strong enough to see a river in position against this lead, as folding would be too tight given the current tournament dynamics and my chip lead.

My hand (Ace-high) is extremely weak on a board of K-K-T-4-2. My opponent called a turn bet, which indicates a range that likely contains at least a Ten, a pocket pair, or a King. Betting as a bluff is unlikely to succeed against this calling range, and my hand has virtually no showdown value. Checking is the most prudent action to avoid losing additional chips while being ahead of very few hands that the opponent might have.

The river pairs the King, which is generally a good card for me as it reduces the likelihood that Flashlight has one. Flashlight bet the turn but has now checked the river. My pocket nines have significant showdown value here against missed draws (like QJ or club draws) and smaller pocket pairs. I don't expect many better hands to fold to a bet, nor many worse hands to call. Therefore, checking back to take the pot at showdown is the most consistent and lowest-risk play to preserve my chip lead.

My hand is 3-5 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and my stack at 9650, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with such a poor hand. I will fold and conserve my stack.

With the button folding, we have a blind vs. blind situation. Q7s is a perfectly reasonable hand to open with from the Small Blind to apply pressure to the Big Blind and try to steal the pot. A standard raise to 3x (300) achieves this while still leaving us with post-flop playability and chips behind if called, as our stack of ~22.5 BB needs to find spots to accumulate chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 5d. The Button has folded and the Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Pro is the short stack with only 2,000 chips remaining. I need to call 200 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 600, giving me 3-to-1 pot odds. 8-5 suited is a reasonable hand to defend in a blind-versus-blind situation, especially with the pot odds offered and my massive chip lead. I will call to see the flop and potentially pressure the short stack if the board texture allows.

We completely missed the flop with Queen-high and only have a backdoor flush draw. The Ace on the board connects well with the Big Blind's calling range, making a continuation bet less effective and risky given our stack size. Checking out of position is the safest option here, allowing us to evaluate the opponent's action and easily fold if facing a bet.

I have an inside straight draw (needing a 2) and a backdoor flush draw on an Ace-high board. Pro raised pre-flop from the Small Blind but checked the flop. This check often indicates weakness or a desire to pot control with a marginal hand. Given Pro's relatively short stack (2,000 chips), a bet of 300 (half the pot) puts significant pressure on their non-Ace hands while allowing me to take control of the pot. If called, I still have my straight draw as an out.

We completely missed the flop and have no direct equity against the Big Blind's betting range. The Ace connects well with many hands they might defend pre-flop, and their bet indicates strength or a draw that has us crushed. With our short stack of 20 BBs, floating or bluff-raising here is highly negative expected value. Folding is the only sensible option.

With AJo on the button and 20 BBs, I have a strong hand. A standard 3x raise is a solid play to extract value from weaker hands and take control of the pot. If I face heavy resistance, I still have room to evaluate.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 3h. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five suited. The button (Pro) has raised to 300. Given the late stage of the tournament and my stack size, a call is a standard and safe play to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five of diamonds on a flop of Ten-Ace-Nine of hearts. I have no pair and no heart flush draw, meaning I have minimal equity against a range that opened from the button. Checking is the standard play here to see how the opponent acts; if they bet, I will likely fold unless I have a good reason to continue.

On a monotone flop of Th Ah 9h, my As Jc gives me top pair but without a heart, my hand is very vulnerable. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands (like made flushes, two pairs, or stronger Aces) or hands with massive equity (like big heart draws). Checking behind for pot control is the safest and most standard line, allowing me to evaluate the turn while keeping the pot size manageable given my relatively short stack.

The board has four hearts (Th, Ah, 9h, Qh), making it a very dangerous texture. I have Jack-high with the Jack of Diamonds and no hearts, which means I have no made hand, no flush, and no straight. Given that the opponent has checked both the flop and the turn, they might be trapping with a flush or also holding a weak hand. Betting into a four-flush board with nothing is a losing strategy. Checking is the only safe option to see the river, after which I will fold to any significant bet.

On a 4-card monotone board with four hearts to the Ace (9h, Th, Qh, Ah), my hand (As Jc) is essentially just a pair of Aces with no flush draw. Betting here has no value, as Flashlight will only continue with a made flush or a stronger hand like two pair or a straight. Checking behind is the only logical choice to control the pot size and avoid value-owning myself against a heart.
The river has been checked around by both players, so the hand proceeds to showdown. Since I have already checked, I will confirm the check to conclude the hand.

With a 4-flush on the board (Ah Qh Th 9h) and no heart in my hand, my As Jc is essentially a bluff-catcher or a marginal showdown hand. Flashlight has checked all three streets, indicating he likely doesn't have a strong heart, but betting here has zero value. He would likely only call with a heart, a straight, or a stronger Ace, all of which beat me. We already beat all of his non-heart missed draws and lower pairs. Checking behind is the only sensible play to realize our showdown value safely.

I am on the button with 3c 5s, which is a very weak starting hand. Given that I have a significant chip lead and we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 147 of 150), there is no strategic reason to risk chips with a bottom-tier hand, even in position. Preserving my stack to secure the win is the priority.
I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind. The pot is 150, and it costs me 50 to complete. Given the favorable pot odds, I will call to see a flop despite holding a weak hand.

I am in the Big Blind with J8o and the Small Blind has limped. J8o is a marginal hand, not strong enough to raise for value or as a bluff against a limping range, especially with my short stack. I will simply check and evaluate the flop in position.

I have flopped bottom two pair (Jacks and Deuces) on a board that features a flush draw. With the opponent (Pro) checking, I should bet to protect my hand against potential flush draws and extract value from hands like a King or lower pair.

I have middle pair with J8o on a Ks Jd 2d board. The Small Blind limped pre-flop, meaning his range is wide and likely capped. His lead of 150 could easily be a diamond flush draw, a straight draw like QT, or a weaker pair, as well as some marginal Kings. Folding second pair here in a limped pot is too weak, but raising would overplay our hand and isolate us against better hands like a King or a stronger Jack. Calling is the optimal play to keep his bluffs and weaker hands in the pot, and we can evaluate on the turn.

With the turn checked through by both players, we are now on the river. I have two pair (Jacks and Deuces) on a board of [2d, Jd, Ks, 6s] and an unknown river card. Since I do not know the river card and my two pair is vulnerable but decent, checking is the most cautious and strategic way to evaluate the opponent's action and potentially reach a showdown.

With middle pair and a weak kicker on a draw-heavy board (two flush draws, straight draws), my hand has showdown value but is rarely strong enough to bet for value after the Small Blind led the flop and checked the turn. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, keep his weaker hands and missed draws in his range, and safely evaluate the river.

I hold two pair (Jacks and Deuces) on a board of [2d, Jd, Ks, 6s, 6h]. The board has paired sixes, which makes any six or better hand superior to my two pair. Given that the turn and river were checked through, my hand has some showdown value, but betting would likely only get called by hands that have me beaten (trips or better). Therefore, checking is the optimal strategy to reach showdown without risking more chips against a potential stronger hand.

On the river, the board pairs the 6. Our hand is Jacks and Sixes. The opponent's line (bet flop, check turn, check river) indicates a lot of missed draws (diamonds, spades, straight draws) or marginal made hands. A value bet here is very thin because worse made hands (like a pair of 2s or underpairs) will likely fold, and we isolate ourselves against hands that beat us, like a King or a better Jack. Checking behind to realize our showdown value is the safest and most optimal play.

Pocket sevens is a strong hand in a three-handed game. With 77, I want to build a pot or take down the blinds pre-flop. Raising to 300 is a standard move given the previous betting patterns observed in the tournament.

Calling leaves us out of position with a marginal hand and allows the Big Blind (who has a massive stack) to squeeze. Shoving could work as a re-steal, but K9s is often dominated by the calling range. With only three hands remaining in the tournament and holding a significant chip lead, my primary objective is to avoid unnecessary risk. Holding 9h-6c in the Big Blind against a 3x open from the second-largest stack (Flashlight) is not a situation that warrants a defense. The hand is weak, and I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. Preserving my stack to ensure a first-place finish is more important than defending a single blind with a sub-par hand.

I am on the Button with a marginal suited connector (5c 7c) and a short stack of 23 BBs (2300). With only two hands left in the tournament, my goal is to maximize my final stack. Opening this hand runs the risk of getting 3-bet or called by the big stacks, forcing me to play a speculative hand post-flop. Folding preserves my stack and avoids unnecessary negative-EV situations.

With only two hands remaining in the tournament and being the significant chip leader, I want to maintain pressure while avoiding unnecessary high-risk confrontations. Folded to me in the Small Blind, Ten-Eight offsuit is a strong enough hand to raise for a steal attempt against the Big Blind. Flashlight has a medium stack and may be hesitant to engage without a premium hand this late in the tournament, especially with the short-stacked Pro still in the game. I will raise to 300 to take down the pot immediately or play a controlled pot out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5h, a suited connector. The chip leader, Flash, has raised to 300 from the small blind. I need to call 200 to see a flop in a pot of 400, offering 2:1 pot odds. Given the position and the fact that we are in the penultimate hand of the tournament, this is a reasonable price to see a flop with a hand that has good potential to hit a flush or straight, despite being out of position.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the significant chip leader with only two hands remaining in the tournament. The flop of Q-7-3 rainbow is relatively dry and likely missed much of the Big Blind's calling range. By placing a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot), I represent a strong hand like a Queen or a pocket pair. This pressure should force a fold from most of Flashlight's air or weak hands. If Flashlight calls or raises, I can comfortably check-fold the turn, as my priority is to maintain my stack for the final hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5h on a board of 7h 3d Qc. The chip leader, Flash, has led out with a bet of 300 into a pot of 600. My hand is currently 9-high with no pair, no straight draw, and only a weak backdoor flush draw. There is no significant equity to justify a call here against a player who has shown aggression throughout the tournament. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my remaining stack for the final hand.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings on the Button for the final hand of the tournament. This is an elite starting hand and I want to maximize value. With the short stack in the Big Blind and a medium stack in the Small Blind, a standard raise is appropriate to build the pot. I am prepared to call any shove from either opponent.

Holding Ace-Trey offsuit in the small blind against a button raise is a marginal spot. Given the positional disadvantage and the weak kicker, this hand does not play well post-flop against a wider button opening range. With a comfortable stack of 9150, there is no need to gamble on a weak hand in the final hand of the tournament. Therefore, folding is the disciplined choice.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em.
